# monster-swarm — security model & audit

This is the security boundary for the distributed-inference guardrails: a holder
of an `agent/*` capability can create, update, and reap leased subagents.
Getting this surface right matters because **`spawn` runs code**.

## Threat model

- **Asset:** the ability to launch and control sandboxed subagent processes on
  the host, and the integrity of the fleet ledger.
- **Primary adversary:** a caller presenting a capability token — possibly
  over-scoped, replayed, revoked, or forged.
- **Secondary adversary:** a compromised or buggy *node* the fleet launched
  (constrained by the monster-guard sandbox + lease).
- **Out of scope:** a root-equipped local attacker (they own `root.key` and the
  host); kernel/sandbox escapes; supply-chain of the node binaries themselves.

## Trust boundaries & controls

| Boundary | Control |
| :--- | :--- |
| Who may mutate the fleet | **UCAN**: `verify_chain` (signature + attenuation + expiry + revocation), must be **rooted at the shared resident root**, leaf must grant `agent/<verb>` on `agent:fleet` (`agent/*` = all). Read ops (`list`, `vibe`, `reconcile`) are local-only. |
| How large the fleet may grow | **AntColony FRF budget**: a manifold population ceiling (default DECAGON = 10); spawn beyond it is denied. |
| What may be torn down | **Two-queen floor**: the last persistent breeder cannot be reaped, so the colony always survives. |
| What may be launched | **Node allowlist**: `spawn` resolves the node against `$MONSTER_SWARM_NODES` / `$DI_BIN_DIR` (+ the DI build dirs under cargo). The resolved path must canonicalize *inside* an allowlisted directory; `..` and arbitrary absolute paths are rejected. |
| What a node may do at runtime | **monster-guard**: macOS `sandbox-exec` profile from the granted caps (fs/net/exec) + an auto-renewing **lease** that re-checks revocation each tick and kills on revoke/expiry/ceiling. |
| Subagent identity | Each subagent has its own **DID**; its secret lives in a `0600` keyfile, never in the ledger or the environment. |
| Ledger integrity | `fleet.json` is `0600` and written **atomically** (temp + rename); `reconcile` prunes zombie rows so the ledger can't drift into orphaned/leaked agents. |

No subprocess is run through a shell — all arguments are passed as argv vectors,
so there is no shell-injection surface. Subagent ids and guard names are
restricted to `[A-Za-z0-9_-]` (≤64), so they cannot escape the grants/keyfile
directories.

## Findings & remediations (this audit)

| # | Severity | Finding | Remediation |
| :- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **High** | `--node` accepted any path ⇒ `agent/spawn` was effectively arbitrary code execution (sandboxed, but any binary). | Node **allowlist** (`guard::resolve_node`): canonicalize and require containment in an allowlisted dir; reject `..` and out-of-tree paths. Secure by default (no allowlist ⇒ nothing resolves). |
| 2 | Medium | MCP `stdout` was polluted by the guard subprocess's stdout, corrupting the JSON-RPC channel. | `guard` delegate/run/revoke now null their stdout; the detached node's stdio goes to its log file. |
| 3 | Medium | Per-subagent secrets / identity did not exist (the guard grant was the only identity). | Per-subagent **DID** + `0600` keyfile; secret never in the ledger or env (only the keyfile *path* is passed). |
| 4 | Medium | Ledger could drift (a crashed supervisor left a zombie row) and non-atomic writes could corrupt it. | `reconcile` prunes dead supervisors (+ removes their keyfiles); `save_fleet` is atomic (temp + rename) and `0600`. |
| 5 | Low | Invalid capability strings passed straight through to the guard. | `validate_caps` rejects anything but `fs`/`net`/`exec` early (guard re-validates as the authority). |
| 6 | Low | `update --caps` implied it could tighten a running node. | Documented honestly: the sandbox is fixed at spawn; cap changes apply on next launch — reap + respawn to tighten a live agent. |

## Accepted residual risks

- **pid reuse** — `reconcile` uses `kill(pid,0)` liveness; a reused pid can mask a
  dead agent. Mitigated by short leases (monster-guard kills unsupervised nodes)
  and by the supervisor being our direct child.
- **Concurrent mutation** — atomic writes prevent corruption, but two
  simultaneous mutators can still lose an update. The design assumes a single
  writer (one resident / one operator); a file lock would close this fully.
- **Environment inheritance** — launched nodes inherit the launcher's
  environment. Run the resident with a minimal env if it holds unrelated
  secrets; monster-swarm adds only `MONSTER_AGENT_*` (no secret values).
- **Unsigned nodes run unsandboxed** — macOS only sandboxes signed binaries;
  monster-guard runs unsigned allowlisted nodes without the seatbelt profile
  (lease + revoke still apply). Sign node binaries for full enforcement.
- **Capability granularity** — `agent/spawn` does not yet constrain *which*
  allowlisted node or *which* caps; that is the allowlist + operator policy
  today. Encoding node/cap constraints in the capability is a future tightening.
